coredata.dc:title: Effective photocatalytic degradation of anthropogenic dyes using graphene oxide grafting titanium dioxide nanoparticles under UV-light irradiation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Jebakumar Immanuel Edison, Thomas Nesakumar
Perumal, Suguna
Karthikeyan, Dhanapalan
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description:
Graphene oxide grafting titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2-GO nanocomposite) was successfully synthesized by a simple solvothermal method. The synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite were systematically characterized by various physico-chemical techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Raman spectroscopy. The XRD results confirm the crystallinity of synthesized bare titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs), pristine graphene oxide (GO) and TiO2-GO nanocomposite with high pure in nature. The average size of the bare TiO2 NPs was around 5nm and were dispersed over the wrinkled graphene layers. Raman spectrum shows the resulting GO and TiO2-GO nanocomposite exhibit moderate graphitization with the intensity of D to G value was 1.1 and 1.2, respectively. The chemical state, functionality and composition (carbon, oxygen and titanium) of the resulting TiO2-GO nanocomposite were revealed by XPS analysis. The photocatalytic activity of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite was investigated on the degradation of hazardous organic dyes (methylene blue (MB) and methyl orange (MO)) under UV-light irradiation and was compared with bare TiO2 NPs and were presented based on the preferred propagation path of induced electrons that leads to generation of O2
─. The resulting TiO2-GO nanocomposite achieve a maximum degradation efficiency of 100 and 84% on MB and MO in a neutral solution within 25 and 240min, respectively under UV-light irradiation, the results show that the GO plays an important role in the enhancement of photocatalytic performance. The high photocatalytic efficiency due to the increased light absorption, the reduced charge recombination with the introduction of GO. Moreover, the simple and affordable solvothermal derived TiO2-GO nanocomposite exhibit rapid photocatalytic degradation on MB in 25min of UV-light irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Titanium dioxide
Methylene blue
Methyl orange
Photocatalyst
Dye degradation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of synthesized pristine GO, bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite
Photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite
Photocatalytic activity of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite
MB degradation efficiency in presence of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite
MO degradation in presence of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology ( 2012M3A7B4049675 ). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) ( NRF-2014R1A2A1A11052391 ) and Priority Research Centers Program ( 2014R1A6A1031189 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of TiO2-GO nanocomposite and degradation process of MB and MO.

XRD pattern of (a) graphite powder and pristine GO, (b) bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite, (c) FTIR spectra of synthesized pristine GO, bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite and (d) Raman spectra of synthesized GO and TiO2-GO nanocomposite.
(a)–(d) FESEM images with different magnification and (e) EDX spectrum of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs.
FESEM images of (a)–(c) synthesized pristine GO and (d)–(f) TiO2-GO nanocomposite with different magnifications.
HRTEM images of synthesized (a)–(c) pristine GO and (d)–(l) TiO2-GO nanocomposite with different magnifications (inset (c): SAED pattern of the corresponding graphene layer for pristine GO).
(a) HAADF-STEM image and the corresponding EDX elemental mapping of (b) carbon, (c) oxygen, (d) titanium, and (e) EDX spectrum of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite.
(a) XPS survey scan spectrum, high resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) Ti 2p and (d) O 1s level of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite.
Photocatalytic degradation mechanism of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite on organic dyes under UV-light irradiation.

UV–vis spectra of MB degradation in the presence of (a) TiO2-GO nanocomposite and (b) bare TiO2 NPs depends on irradiation time, (c) catalytic efficiency (%) on MB degradation at different irradiation time and (d) first order kinetics plot of ln(At/A0) versus irradiation time for MB degradation.

UV–vis spectra of MO degradation in the presence of (a) TiO2-GO nanocomposite and (b) bare TiO2 NPs depends on irradiation time, (c) catalytic efficiency (%) on MO degradation at different irradiation time and (d) first order kinetics plot of ln(At/A0) versus irradiation time for MO degradation.

Photographic images of (a) MB solution, MB in the presence of (b) TiO2-GO nanocomposite and (c) bare TiO2 NPs after 25min of UV-light irradiation; (d) MO solution, MO in the presence of (e) TiO2-GO nanocomposite and (f) bare TiO2 NPs after 240min of UV-light irradiation.
Comparison of photocatalytic activity of graphene grafted TiO2 nanoparticles based nanocomposite on MB degradation.

head.ce:title: Effective photocatalytic degradation of anthropogenic dyes using graphene oxide grafting titanium dioxide nanoparticles under UV-light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Arignar Anna College of Arts and Science, Krishnagiri 635 001, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Arignar Anna College of Arts and Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Graphene oxide grafting titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2-GO nanocomposite) was successfully synthesized by a simple solvothermal method. The synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite were systematically characterized by various physico-chemical techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Raman spectroscopy. The XRD results confirm the crystallinity of synthesized bare titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs), pristine graphene oxide (GO) and TiO2-GO nanocomposite with high pure in nature. The average size of the bare TiO2 NPs was around 5nm and were dispersed over the wrinkled graphene layers. Raman spectrum shows the resulting GO and TiO2-GO nanocomposite exhibit moderate graphitization with the intensity of D to G value was 1.1 and 1.2, respectively. The chemical state, functionality and composition (carbon, oxygen and titanium) of the resulting TiO2-GO nanocomposite were revealed by XPS analysis. The photocatalytic activity of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite was investigated on the degradation of hazardous organic dyes (methylene blue (MB) and methyl orange (MO)) under UV-light irradiation and was compared with bare TiO2 NPs and were presented based on the preferred propagation path of induced electrons that leads to generation of O2
─. The resulting TiO2-GO nanocomposite achieve a maximum degradation efficiency of 100 and 84% on MB and MO in a neutral solution within 25 and 240min, respectively under UV-light irradiation, the results show that the GO plays an important role in the enhancement of photocatalytic performance. The high photocatalytic efficiency due to the increased light absorption, the reduced charge recombination with the introduction of GO. Moreover, the simple and affordable solvothermal derived TiO2-GO nanocomposite exhibit rapid photocatalytic degradation on MB in 25min of UV-light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal oxide semiconductor is a suitable photocatalyst for the degradation of organic dyes because of their photocatalytic activity. Among the semiconductor photocatalysts, titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) has attracted numerous interests because of its excellent physicochemical properties, such as suitable band position, non-toxicity, low cost, chemical and photonic stability, and biocompatibility [1,2]. In general, titanium dioxide (TiO2) exists in three crystallographic forms in nature: brookite, anatase and rutile. Among them, anatase plays a dominant role in photocatalytic applications due the superior photocatalytic efficiency and very facile synthesis. However, the photocatalytic efficiency on a bare TiO2 remains quite limited, due to the rapid recombination rate of photogenerated electron–hole pairs within TiO2 particles. Hence, in recent days, graphene based semiconducting nanocomposite (graphene oxide (GO) and TiO2) pay a way for solving the problems of electron-hole recombination, narrow light absorption bands, and shows high affinity towards photocatalytic reactions of TiO2 NPs. The graphene layers adhered to TiO2 NPs retrieve the induced electrons for photocatalytic reactions and prevent recombination with the photogenerated holes [3–5]. Various methods have been developed to prepare graphene based semiconducting photocatalyst includes TiO2-GO nanocomposite for improve the structure stability and photocatalytic activity [6,7]. Among various methods of preparing TiO2-GO nanocomposite, solvothermal process finds an efficient, very simple and economic route followed by heating at 65°C.

The photocatalytic process is considered as one of the most promising and green technologies for eliminating organic contaminants because of the high efficiency for mineralization of dyes by light irradiation. This process can mineralize organic dyes to H2O, CO2, and other nontoxic inorganic compounds without causing a secondary pollution. Organics dyes such as methylene blue (MB) and methyl orange (MO) are widely used in various industries and this has created a serious environmental problem for water pollution, particularly due to the severe color and the high oxygen demand, causing great harms to the aquatic organisms and the environment [8–10]. In particular, Azo-dyes represent the most common dyes actually employed in textile industries which environmentally show toxic effects, especially carcinogenic and mutagenic events [11,12]. Therefore, the degradation of hazardous dye is one of the significant issues all over the world. A considerable amount of research has been conducted in the past several decades for the effective treatment of dye degradation [13,14].

In the present work, we report the investigation in the treatment of MB and MO by using synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite as a photocatalyst for the degradation in neutral aqueous solutions under ultraviolet-light (UV-light) irradiation. The bare TiO2 NPs and GO were synthesized by simple sol-gel and modified Hummers method, respectively. The synthesized GO (graphene layers) was grafted over the TiO2 NPs by simple and economic solvothermal process. The photocatalytic efficiency of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite on MB and MO degradation was examined and compared with bare TiO2 NPs. In addition, the photocatalytic performance of TiO2-GO nanocomposite on MB degradation (decolouration) compared with on MO. The optical property of entire experiment was revealed by ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy techniques. The resulting TiO2-GO nanocomposite achieves a maximum degradation efficiency as compared to bare TiO2 NPs due to the increased light absorption, the reduced charge recombination with the introduction of GO.

Graphite powder, titanium (IV) isopropoxide (99.99%), anhydrous ethanol (99.5%), MB and MO were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Potassium permanganate, barium chloride, hydrogen peroxide, sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, tetrahydrofuran and acetone were purchased from Ducksan chemicals. All chemicals were used without any further purification and deionized (DI) water was used to prepare solutions.

The GO was synthesized using the modified Hummers method without any major modification [15]. The entire synthesis procedure was adopted from our previous reported work [16]. The resulted GO was used for further synthesis of TiO2-GO nanocomposite.

TiO2 NPs were synthesized from titanium (IV) isopropoxide by the simple sol-gel method. In a typical synthesis procedure, 25mL of absolute alcohol and 25mL of DI water was taken in to a beaker. 9mL of titanium (IV) isopropoxide in 20mL of anhydrous ethanol was added drop wise into the above mixture under vigorous stirring. The mixture was stirred for 60min at room temperature under ambient condition. 1M H2SO4 was added drop wise in to reaction mixture until it reach the pH 3.5 and then stirred for additional 60min. The final mixture was centrifuged, washed with DI water several time and then the residue was dried in a hot air oven at 70°C for 5h. The dried (as-synthesized) TiO2 NPs was calcined at 400°C for 4h in order to remove the water molecules and the hydroxyl group over the surface and core of the TiO2 NPs. The final bare TiO2 NPs was stored in a glass vial for further use.

An appropriate amount (500mg) of GO was dispersed in 75mL of tetrahydrofuran in a 100mL round bottom (RB) flask. 200mg of bare TiO2 NPs were added in to the pristine GO dispersion and sonicated for 20min at ambient condition, then the dispersed reaction mixture was refluxed at 65°C for 24h under static condition. The final homogeneous mixture was centrifuged and washed with tetrahydrofuran. The obtained residue was dried at 100°C for 5h in a hot air oven under static condition. The resulting final product was collected and used to enhance the photocatalytic activity on degradation of organic dyes including MB and MO. The schematic representation of the synthesis of TiO2-GO nanocomposite and degradation process of MB and MO were shown in Fig. 1 .

The synthesized bare TiO2 NPs, GO and TiO2-GO nanocomposite were characterized by various physico-chemical techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy-attenuated total reflectance (ATR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), UV–vis spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy. The XRD patterns were obtained on a PANalytical X’Pert diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (K=1.54Å) equipped with a liquid nitrogen cooled germanium solid-state detector. The XRD patterns of resulted samples were recorded in the 2θ range of 10–90° at the step interval of 0.02° with the counting time of 5s at each point. The ATR-FTIR spectra were recorded in transmittance mode on a perkin elmer spectrum two in the wavenumber range of 400–4000cm−1 by the co-addition of 32 scans at a resolution of 16cm−1. Raman spectra were recorded with a Raman spectrometer Almega X/Thermo using laser excitation line at 532nm. FESEM was performed on Hitachi S-4800 at an accelerating voltage of 4kV. The preparation of samples for FESEM, a small amount of samples was placed on conductive carbon tape. HRTEM images were obtained using a TITAN G2 ChemiSTEM Cs Probe electron microscope operated at 200kV. Samples for HRTEM were prepared by placing droplets of a suspension sample in ethanol on a carbon-coated polymer micro grid supported on a Cu grid. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). The spectra were baseline corrected using the instrument software. UV–vis spectroscopy analysis was performed using an OPTIZEN 3220 UV.

Photocatalytic activity of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite were tested on MB and MO in neutral aqueous solutions under UV-light irradiation. The degradation efficiency of synthesized photocatalyst was examined by using UV–vis spectrophotometer. The MB dye solution was prepared in DI water with absorbance intensity of around 1.6 for the photocatalytic degradation experiments. 50mL of prepared MB dye solution was taken into a 100mL beaker and kept on the magnetic stirrer. Subsequently, 20mg of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite was added into the reactant and reaction was carried out under UV-light (wavelength 365nm, distance 100mm, power 40W) irradiation with mild stirring until the decolouration of MB dye. UV–vis measurement was carried for every 5min to find the absorbance of reaction mixture until complete of the reaction (until decolorize the MB dye). After 25min, the reaction mixture (MB dye) was decolorized completely. As control experiment, MB dye with bare TiO2 NPs in order to find the degradation efficiency (%) of TiO2-GO nanocomposite. Similar reaction condition was adopted for the degradation of MB dye in the presence of bare TiO2 NPs (TiO2-GO nanocomposite replaced by bare TiO2 NPs). And also similar reaction conditions were adopted for the photocatalytic activity of MO dye in the presence of photocatalyst (synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite). 50mL of MO dye was used instead of MB dye and reaction was carried out under UV-light irradiation. UV–vis measurement was carried for every 30min to find the absorbance in 240min the reaction mixture become colorless. The degradation efficiency (%) of MB and MO dyes was calculated and compared with synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized pristine GO, bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite

ce:section.ce:para: The wide-angle XRD pattern of graphite powder and pristine GO are presented in Fig. 2 (a). From the XRD patterns, the peaks position at 26.1, 42, 44, 54° (2θ) were associated with the C(002), C(100), C(101) and C(004) diffractions plane of the hexagonal graphite structure for raw graphite powder. The peaks at 2θ values of 11.5 and 42.2°can be perfectly assigned to the planes of the C(002) and C(100) of the hexagonal graphite structure for synthesized pristine GO. The peak position of the C(002) plane for GO was shifted to the lower 2θ values, which is due to oxidation of graphite powder and the interlayer distance has increased from ∼0.34 to 0.76nm [17]. From these results we concluded that the oxidation of graphite powder to GO was occurred successfully without contamination [18].

Fig. 2(b) shows the wide angle XRD pattern of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite. The XRD pattern of bare TiO2 NPs, the peaks at 2θ values of 24.8, 35.7, 37.5, 47.4, 53.7, 54.4, 62.2, 68.5, 69.4, 74.8 and 82.3°can be perfectly assigned to the crystal planes of the (101), (103), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220), (215) and (303) of the body-centered tetragonal TiO2 which were in good agreement with the standard JCPDS card no. 84-1285. The XRD pattern indicates that the synthesized nanomaterial is anatase TiO2 without a significant amount of any other phases such as rutile or brookite [19]. The main intense peaks of TiO2 NPs in the XRD pattern are broadened, indicating the crystalline portion of the TiO2 NPs is very small [20]. The average size of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs was calculated from the XRD pattern according to the line width of the (101) plane refraction peak using Debye-Scherrer equation. The average size of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs was 5.5nm. The d-spacing value of synthesized TiO2 NPs determined from the (101) reflection of the XRD pattern using Bragg law is found to be ca. 0.35nm. From the XRD patterns of TiO2-GO nanocomposite, the peaks at 2θ values of 11.5 and 42.2° were associated with the C(002) and C(100) diffractions of the hexagonal graphite structure, similar to synthesized pristine GO, further TiO2 NPs patterns observed as accordance with the JCPDS card no. 84-1285 similar to synthesized bare TiO2 NPs. In addition, the average size of TiO2 NPs on the surface of graphene layer calculated using the Debye-Scherrer equation based on the full width at half-maximum of the (101) diffraction plane is about 5nm. The average size of the TiO2 NPs on the surface graphene layer was little smaller than synthesized bare TiO2 NPs, this might due to splitting of bigger nanoparticles during the solvothermal process of TiO2-GO nanocomposite. Moreover, the XRD results suggest that the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite exhibit crystalline with high pure.

Fig. 2(c) shows the ATR-FTIR spectra of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs, GO and TiO2-GO nanocomposite. From the FTIR spectrum of bare TiO2 NPs, the strong absorption band 1100cm−1 and below 1000cm−1 was attributed to the typical skeletal vibration of TiOTi bonds in TiO2 NPs [21], and the broad absorption band appearing at approximately 3200cm−1 clearly showed the vibration of TiOH or physically adsorbed water molecules on the surface of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs. The results reveal that the synthesized TiO2 NPs are pure and shows good agreement with literature results [22]. The presence of absorption bands around 1725cm−1 and 1390cm−1 for the synthesized pristine GO corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO and CO group, respectively. The absorption band appeared at 1575cm−1 due to the CC (sp2-hybridized carbon atoms) stretching vibration of graphitic structure. The characteristic bands at 1245, 1040 and 585cm−1 confirm the presence of the epoxy groups complying with the symmetric stretching, asymmetric stretching, and deformation vibrations, respectively [23,24]. The broad absorption band around 3200cm−1 due to the OH stretching vibration of the physically absorbed water molecules or functionalization of hydroxyl groups over the synthesized materials is observed [25]. The FTIR spectrum of the TiO2-GO nanocomposite are parent to the GO spectrum but the intensity of the OH and CO (carbonyl group) stretching vibration band slightly decreased compared to GO powder. This might be due to tiny TiO2 NPs occupied over the graphene layers and GO partially reduced to graphene sheets during the solvothermal process for fabrication of TiO2-GO nanocomposite. In addition, the absorption intensity of CC slightly increased for TiO2-GO nanocomposite which supports the partially reduction of GO during the solvothermal process. The typical skeletal vibration of TiOTi bonds corresponds to the TiO2 NPs merged with GO spectrum are clear from the spectrum of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite. Moreover, the absence of TiOC bonds, confirms the absence chemical bonding between GO and TiO2 NPs within TiO2-GO nanocomposite during the solvothermal process. Thus, FTIR spectroscopy analysis confirms that TiO2 NPs physically anchored over the graphene layers. After photocatalytic degradation, the utilized TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite photocatalysts were recovered, and the changes in the functional groups over the TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite were analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. S1 shows the FTIR spectra of reminisced TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite (after photocatalytic degradation of MB and MO) which is similar as that of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite (more details see the Supporting information).

Raman spectroscopy provides the information about the construction of the carbon related material by examining the sp2 and sp3 hybridization. The sp2 hybridization is related to the presence of the aromatic ring from graphitic structure. The sp3 hybridized carbon atoms related to the lattice defects in the graphitic structure [26,27]. Fig. 2(d) shows the Raman spectra of the synthesized pristine GO and TiO2-GO nanocomposite. The pristine GO spectra show two bands at 1345 and 1596cm−1 , corresponding to the D-band and G-band, respectively. The D-band associated with vibrations of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, and the G-band associated with vibrations of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the graphitic structure [28,29]. The peak intensities of the D-band and G-band are denoted as ID and IG , respectively. The graphitization of materials was confirmed by the value of ID/IG and the value is approximately 1.1 and 1.2 for pristine GO powder and TiO2-GO nanocomposite, respectively. The intensity ratio of D to G band (ID
>
IG ) indicates that the obtained materials were moderate graphitization due to surface defects of the GO. The ID/IG ratio of TiO2-GO nanocomposite little higher than GO powder which clearly demonstrates that the small increment of lattice defects in the graphene structure of TiO2-GO nanocomposite. These lattice defects will be favour for well dispersion of TiO2 NPs and also for stronger interaction between the TiO2 NPs and graphene layers [30]. Furthermore, the D-band and G-band were slightly shifted to higher wave numbers for TiO2-GO nanocomposite compared to GO (the wave number of D-band and G-band about 1350 and 1600cm−1, respectively). This might be due to the presence of large-size surfaces in the material and also presence of small pieces of thin graphene layers because of sonication and solvothermal process. In addition, Raman spectra was used to investigate the crystalline phase of TiO2 in the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite. Three band observed at 151, 399 and 640cm−1 in the Raman spectra of the resulting TiO2-GO nanocomposite, which are well consistent with anatase TiO2 [31]. These result confirm that the synthesized TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite has anatase phase TiO2.

The surface morphology and elemental composition of the synthesized TiO2 NPs was revealed by FESEM with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy analysis is shown in Fig. 3 . The synthesized bare TiO2 NPs exhibits a near spherical shape with aggregation of tiny crystals are clear (Fig. 3(a–d)). EDX spectrum of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs is presented in Fig. 3(e). The EDX spectrum is used as a quantitative analysis for the presence of components in the synthesized materials. Titanium (Ti) and oxygen (O) only are present in the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs which confirms the obtained TiO2 NPs is highly pure. Fig. 4 shows the respective FESEM images of the synthesized pristine GO (Fig. 4(a–c)) and TiO2-GO nanocomposite (Fig. 4(d–f)) with different magnifications. The synthesized pristine GO exhibited few-layered sheet-like structure with different orientations with smooth morphology. From the FESEM images of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite, the smooth surface morphology slightly moved to rigid nature by the addition of TiO2 NPs. The TiO2 NPs are not clearly observed in the images due to low magnification as well as tiny nature of TiO2 NPs. The surface defect of the synthesized materials was slightly increased by the addition of bare TiO2 NPs which was confirmed by Raman studies.

The morphology of the synthesized pristine GO (Fig. 5 (a–c)) and TiO2-GO nanocomposite (Fig. 5(d–l)) were investigated by HRTEM analysis. The few-layered thin and wrinkled graphene sheets were observed from low magnification HRTEM images (Fig. 5(a)). In addition, the low magnificent HRTEM image reveals that the sheet of pristine GO is transparent and faintly rolled (wrinkled) and that the wrinkles are primarily located on the edge of the GO. The lattice fringes of graphene clearly seen in high magnification HRTEM images. Inset of Fig. 5(c) shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of corresponding graphene layer of pristine GO. Two bright rings (patterns) observed from SAED corresponds to C(002) and C(100) planes for typical hexagonal graphitic structure [32–34]. The obtained SAED pattern shows good agreement with XRD pattern of GO and this results revealed that the synthesized GO powder is in moderate graphitic nature. The heterostructure of the TiO2-GO nanocomposite can be verified by the morphological analyses which is shown in Fig. 5(d)–(l). It is clearly seen that the almost transparent carbon sheets were decorated randomly by TiO2 NPs and the diameters of the TiO2 NPs is around 5±1nm. HRTEM image clearly shows the lattice fringes of TiO2 NPs and graphene layers and the calculated inter layer distance of TiO2 NPs is approximately 0.35nm which more closer to bulk TiO2 and XRD results (refer to Section 3.1.1) [35]. From the low magnification HRTEM image of TiO2-GO nanocomposite, the dark areas indicate the thick stacking nanostructure of several graphene oxide and/or graphene layers with some amount of oxygen functional groups. The higher transparency areas indicate much thinner films of a few layers graphene oxide and/or reduced graphene oxide resulting from solvothermal process exfoliation [36]. Furthermore, the anchored TiO2 NPs distributed uniformly on the graphene sheet and particles can deposit on both sides of the graphene sheets, which make them excellent candidates for photocatalytic application.

The elemental composition of the resulting materials were revealed by EDX pattern with elemental mapping. High angle annular dark field-scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and EDX spectroscopy with EDX elemental mapping of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite were shown in Fig. 6 . The EDX elemental mapping of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite consisted of carbon (C), oxygen (O) and titanium (Ti) which is clear from Fig. 6(b)–(d). The random dispersion of TiO2 NPs on the graphene oxide layers are clear from EDX elemental mapping. The EDX pattern is used as a quantitative analysis of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite. The EDX pattern (Fig. 6(e)) shows the C, O and Ti elements only present in the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite which revealed that the resulting TiO2-GO nanocomposite is highly pure. Moreover, the results indicate that TiO2 NPs were dispersed well on the surface of graphene layers.

XPS is a surface-sensitive quantitative spectroscopic technique that is a one of the important tools to measures the elemental composition, chemical state and electronic state of the elements that exist within a material. Fig. S2 shows the XPS survey spectrum of the synthesized pristine GO and it showed two predominant binding energy peaks around 285 and 532eV which is corresponding to the C 1s and O 1s, respectively. The absence of any other functionalized groups except carbon and oxygen confirms the synthesized GO possess high purity (more details see the Supporting information). The survey scan XPS recorded from 0 to 1350eV (Fig. 7 (a)) show the three predominant binding energy peaks at 285, 460 and 532eV which were assigned to the C 1s, Ti 2p and O 1s level, whereas the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite. The high resolution XPS of C 1s was presented in Fig. 7(b) and indicates that the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite have a considerable degree of oxidation with different oxygen-containing functional groups. The C 1s spectrum consisted of four binding energy appeared at 284.6, 285.0, 286.8 and 287.4 eV, which correspond to the non-oxygenated ring C atom (CC/CC, attributed to the sp2/sp3 carbon atom), C atom in a CO bond from COC epoxy, C atom in a COH/carbonyl C atom (CO) and C atom in a OCOH functional groups, respectively [37,38]. In addition, the absence of CO–Ti peak in the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite indicate that there is no chemical COTi bond formed between the graphene layers and TiO2 NPs which further supported from the FTIR spectroscopy results (Section 3.1.2). The deconvoluted spectrum of Ti 2p shows two peaks at the binding energy of 459.7 and 465.5eV corresponding to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 levels, respectively (Fig. 7(c)). This indicated that the Ti was present in the form of TiO2 into synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite. The deconvoluted spectrum of O 1s level (Fig. 7(d)) showed two predominant peaks with a binding energy of 530.9 and 532.6eV was associated with the TiO/CO and Ti-OH/COH/CO/OCO, carbonyl oxygen atoms in carboxylic groups on the surface of graphene layers, respectively [39]. The atomic percentage of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite are approximately 62.41, 32.77 and 4.82%, for carbon (C), oxygen (O) and titanium (Ti), respectively which was obtained from the XPS results. The synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite show the main functional groups on the surface graphene layers, COC, COH, CO, OCO additionally, TiO and TOH groups, suggest that the synthesized material are with high purity under our preparation conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite was evaluated by using MB as well as MO as the model compound in a neutral aqueous solution under UV-light irradiation. The photocatalytic mechanism of the TiO2-GO nanocomposite is well known and the schematic illustration of the photocatalytic degradation of MB in presence of TiO2-GO nanocomposite under UV-light irradiation is shown in Fig. 8
[40,41]. A pairs of electrons and holes were produced when photocatalyst TiO2 NPs absorbs UV radiation from UV lamp (light). The electrons of the valence band (VB) of TiO2 becomes excited and then excess energy of this excited electron promoted the electron to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 under UV-light irradiation therefore creating the negative-electron (e−) and positive-hole (h+) pair. This stage is referred as the semiconductor's photo-excitation state. The energy difference between the VB and the CB is known as the band gap. The negative-electron in the VB of TiO2 are easily transferred to CB of graphene oxide layers under UV-light irradiation. The positive-hole breaks apart the water molecule to form hydrogen gas and hydroxyl radical (OH•). The negative-electron reacts with oxygen molecule to form super oxide anion (O2) on the surface of graphene oxide layers to interact with dyes (MB and MO). This cycle continues while UV-light irradiation, thus enhance the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite and the proposed photocatalytic degradation mechanism are clearly illustrated in Fig. 8. The degradation efficiency (%) of organic dye was directly correlated with irradiation time which confirms the UV–vis absorbance. This result demonstrates that the introduction of TiO2 NPs to graphene oxide layers can dramatically enhance the photocatalytic activity through an interfacial interaction between TiO2 NPs and graphene oxide layers. The major reaction steps of the photocatalytic mechanism under UV-light irradiation of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite with dyes are described in the following equations [42]:
TiO
2
+ h v →
e −
( C B ) +
h +
( V B ) →
G
O (
e −
) +

T i
O
2

e −
) +
O
2
→
O
2
⋅ −
+ T i
O
2
→
H
2
O
•
O H + T i
O
2
T i
O
2
(
h +
) + O
H −
→ •
O H + T i
O
2
G
O (

) +
O
2

O
2
−

O

2
O

O H +
G
O
•
O H + D y e ( M B o r M O ) → D e g r a d e d Pr o d u c t

2
• −
+ D y e ( M B o r M O ) → D e g r a d e d Pr o d u c t

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite
MB degradation efficiency in presence of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of GO on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs assembly modified GO nanocomposites, the photocatalytic degradation of MB and or MO over the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite was performed. The photocatalytic activity of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite was examined on MB degradation using UV–vis spectroscopy, MB displays blue color in water and absorb in the visible region at 612 and 664nm. Fig. 9 (a) shows the time dependent UV–vis spectra of MB in presence of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite in a neutral aqueous solution under UV-light irradiation. The absorbance intensity of MB gradually decreases with increasing the irradiation time by the addition of TiO2-GO nanocomposite under UV-light, and decolorize the solution take places after 25min of irradiation and the corresponding degradation efficiency about 100%. Similar MB degradation reaction was carried out in presence of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs. Fig. 9(b) shows the absorbance intensity of MB little decrease with increasing the irradiation time in the presence of bare TiO2 NPs and the nature of MB color was retain after 25min of irradiation. Fig. 9(c) shows the degradation efficiency (%) of MB in the presence of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite under UV-light irradiation at different time intervals based on the equation 1. The rapid degradation of MB was observed in the presence of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite and 100% of the MB dye were degraded within 25 min. The degradation efficiency (%) of MB in presence of TiO2 NPs was 44% after 25min of UV-light irradiation. The degradation efficiency (%) of MB in presence of TiO2-GO nanocomposite was obviously higher than the in presence of bare TiO2 NPs. The degradation efficiency of MB was calculated by pseudo-first order kinetics according to our previous reports [43,44]. The first order kinetics plots of ln(At/A0) versus irradiation time for both of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite on MB degradation, which yield a straight line with the negative slope value as a rate constant are presented in Fig. 9(d). The results suggest that the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs are greatly enhanced after grafting with GO, and thus synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite shows the highest photocatalytic activity with a rate constant (k) of 0.268min−1 (4.47×10−3 s−1), which is 12 times higher than that of bare TiO2 NPs (rate constant of only 0.022 min−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MO degradation in presence of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite was examined on MO degradation under UV-light irradiation. The MO shows orange color in water and strongly absorbed at 466nm in the visible region due to the presence of azo groups. The time dependent UV–vis spectra of MO in presence of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite and bare TiO2 NPs in a neutral aqueous solution under UV-light irradiation are presented in Fig. 10 (a) and (b), respectively. The MO absorbance peak shifted to higher wavenumber region after addition of photocatalyst (synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite) clear from the UV–vis spectra of MB degradation. The absorbance intensity of MO obviously decreases with increasing the irradiation time in presence of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite compared to the bare TiO2 NPs under UV-light. MO was almost decolorized in presence TiO2-GO nanocomposite after 240min of irradiation but in presence TiO2, the MO color was retained even after 240min of irradiation (refer Fig. 11 ). Fig. 10(c) shows the degradation efficiency (%) of MO in presence of TiO2-GO nanocomposite and bare TiO2 NPs at different irradiation time. The degradation of MO occurs moderately and almost completely in presence of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite under UV-light irradiation and achieved 84% after 240min of irradiation, which is higher than that of bare TiO2 NPs (degradation efficiency of only 55%). The photocatalytic reactions of MO also fit the pseudo-first order kinetics. Fig. 10(d) shows the first order kinetics plots of ln(At/A0) versus irradiation time for both of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite on MO degradation, which yield a straight line with the negative slope value as a rate constant. The synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite and bare TiO2 NPs shows the photocatalytic activity with a k of 6.75×10−3 min−1 (1.13×10−4 s−1) and 3.49×10−3 min−1 (5.81×10−5 s−1), respectively. The synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite shows the highest photocatalytic activity of MO with a rate constant is 2 times higher than that of bare TiO2 NPs. These photocatalytic degradation results suggest that the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity for MB and MO degradation, and the degradation efficiency reaches 100 and 84%, respectively after 25 and 240min of UV-light irradiation, respectively compared with photocatalytic activity of bare TiO2 NPs for MB and MO (the degradation efficiency reaches 44 and 55% at 25 and 240min, corresponding to the MB and MO, respectively). Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite on MB with rate constant is about 40 times higher than that of photocatalytic activity on MO. In addition, in order to justify the superior photocatalytic activity of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite, the present study was compared with the recent reports of MB degradation by graphene grafted TiO2 based nanomaterials and results were depicted in Table 1
[45–54].
(1)
Degradation efficiency ( % ) =
A 0
−
A t
A 0
× 100
where, A0 and At is the absorbance of the initial and remnant dye (MB at 664nm and MO at 466nm) concentration, respectively under UV-light irradiation, t is the reaction time in min.

Fig. 11(a) and (d) shows the photographic images of blank MB and MO solution, respectively in absence of photocatalyst (synthesized bare TiO2 NPs and TiO2-GO nanocomposite) before UV-light irradiation. Photographic images of MB in presence of TiO2-GO nanocomposite and bare TiO2 NPs after 25min of UV-light irradiation were presented in Fig. 11(b) and (c), respectively. After 25min of irradiation, MB in presence of TiO2-GO nanocomposite was completely decolorized (colorless) the MB but in presence of bare TiO2 NPs retained the color (Fig. 11(c)). The results suggest that the MB in presence of TiO2-GO nanocomposite completely degraded were as in case of bare TiO2 NPs, MB degrades partially. Fig. 11(e) and (f) shows the MO in presence of TiO2-GO nanocomposite and bare TiO2 NPs, respectively after 240min of UV-light irradiation. MO in presence of TiO2-GO nanocomposite was nearly decolorized and MO in presence of bare TiO2 NPs retains its original MO color which is due to very less photocatalytic activity of synthesized materials on MO degradation. Moreover, the degradation efficiency of synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite on MB is maximum than compared to MO.

The potential TiO2-GO nanocomposite (photocatalyst) was easily synthesized via simple and affordable solvothermal route. The synthesized TiO2 NPs exhibit only an anatase phase revealed from XRD analysis. Raman spectrum confirms the graphitization of synthesized pristine GO and TiO2-GO nanocomposite with the value of ID/IG is approximately 1.1 and 1.2, respectively. The tiny TiO2 NPs with mean size of 5nm were dispersed over the GO layers are clear from HRTEM images. All the characterization techniques strongly support that the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite was constructed well with stable and high pure. The resulting TiO2-GO nanocomposite used as an active photocatalyst for the MB and MO degradation in an aqueous medium under UV-light irradiation. The synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite displayed significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity toward MB and MO degradation in a neutral aqueous solution under UV-light irradiation than that of synthesized bare TiO2 NPs (photocatalyst). The resulting TiO2-GO heterostructure achieves a maximum degradation efficiency of 100% (25min of irradiation) and 84% (240min of irradiation) corresponding to the MB and MO, respectively. The rate constant (k) of MB and MO degradation in presence of the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite was 0.268min−1 and 6.75×10−3 min−1, respectively. The synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite achieves a maximum degradation (%) with excellent rate constant (k) values when compare with recent reports on MB degradation by TiO2 NPs-graphene based nanocomposite. Thus, the synthesized TiO2-GO nanocomposite can be a promising candidate for the degradation of anthropogenic (MB and MO) dyes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (NRF-2014R1A2A1A11052391) and Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2016.10.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
